narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 39, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many students would envy. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants at 92.7%, and your Year 3 school decile ranking of 6 is slightly better than average. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 63.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted failure risk is why your law school application was rejected—the school uses this model to prioritize applicants likely to pass the bar on their first try and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your family income in the middle bracket, which the model sees as a hurdle compared to higher-income backgrounds that often correlate with bar success in the data. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 5—below the average of 5.71—signaled an early academic struggle that weighed heavily against you. Even your above-average LSAT, while helpful, had a smaller counterbalancing effect and wasn't enough to offset those top concerns. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often come from stronger financial situations and show consistent high deciles from the start, your profile highlighted these risks more prominently. This doesn't reflect your full potential or effort, but rather how the model patterns past outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher, strengthening your early-grade record through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications, and exploring scholarships or programs that support middle-income students aiming for law. Many applicants in similar situations improve their profiles and gain admission elsewhere—your UGPA and LSAT already give you a great starting point for that. Keep building on your strengths, and targeted improvements in those key areas could shift the odds dramatically next time.
